coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of carbon quantum dots grafted Co3O4 nanocomposite for supercapacitors application
coredata.dc:creator: Prabakaran, Periyasami
Arumugam, Gowdhaman
Ramu, Perumal
Selvaraj, Manickam
Assiri, Mohammed A.
Rokhum, Samuel Lalthazuala
Periyasamy, Sivakumar
Arjunan, Silambarasan
Rajendran, Ramesh
coredata.dc:description: A pure Co3O4 nanoparticles and a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) anchored cobalt oxide (Co3O4/CQD) nanocomposite were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method for supercapacitor application. The powder XRD measurement confirms the formation cubic phase of Co3O4. More HR-TEM measurements. The beneficial surface and electronic properties of CQDs were used for the refinement of the electrochemical accomplishments of Co3O4. The Co3O4/CQD and Co3O4 manifest half-cell specific capacitances of 193.8 F g −1 and 123.6 F g −1 @ current density of 4 A g −1, respectively. The introduction of CQDs into the Co3O4 matrix increased the charge-transfer process, which is primarily responsible for the improved electrochemical capabilities of the nanocomposite. The hybrid asymmetric supercapacitor delivers an energy density of 2.49 W h kg−1 @ a power density of 426 W kg−2 with a capacitance retention of 81.8%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterails
Metal oxide
Electrode materials
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesis of Co3O4 and fabrication of Co3O4/CQDnanocomposite.
Powder XRD spectra of PCO and COC.
(a) Survey XPS stretch of Co3O4/CQD, High magnification XPS spectra of (b) Co 2p, (c) O 1 s and (d) C 1s.
FESEM images of (a-b) Co3O4, (c-d) Co3O4/CQD at different magnifications.
(a-d) EDS mapping and (e) EDS spectra of COC.
(a-c) HRTEM images at different magnifications (d) SAED pattern, (e) EDX spectrum of COC.

CV curves of (a) PCO, (b) COC at various scan rates, (c) GC/GD curves of (c) PCO, (d) COC at various current densities, (e) Comparison of CVs of PCO & COC at constant scan rate 60 mV s −1, (f) Comparison GC/GD of pure and composite electrodes at 4 A g −1.
Comparison of (a) Specific capacitances. (b) Nyquist plots of PCO & COC. (c) Fitted equivalent circuit of EIS curves. (d) Capacitance retention & Coulombic efficiency of COC.

(a) Schematic diagram of the assembled ASC device, (b) CV curves of r-GO and COC at the scan rate of 10 mV s −1, (c & d) CV curves of the device at different potentials (constant scan rate of 60 mV s −1) and at different scan rates respectively, (e & f) GC/GD curves of the device at various potentials (constant current density of 0.6 A g −1) and different current densities, respectively.

(a) Specific capacitance of ASC. (b) Ragone plots. (c) N-q plots of ASC. (d) Fitted equivalent circuit of Nyquist curve. (e) Capacitance retention & Coulombic efficiency of ASC. (f) A LED lit up by ASC.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of carbon quantum dots grafted Co3O4 nanocomposite for supercapacitors application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: fDepartment of Chemistry, Vel Tech Rangarajan Dr.Sagunthala R&D Institute of Science and Technology, Avadi, Chennai, Tamil Nadu 600 062, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pure Co3O4 nanoparticles and a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) anchored cobalt oxide (Co3O4/CQD) nanocomposite were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method for supercapacitor application. The powder XRD measurement confirms the formation cubic phase of Co3O4. More HR-TEM measurements. The beneficial surface and electronic properties of CQDs were used for the refinement of the electrochemical accomplishments of Co3O4. The Co3O4/CQD and Co3O4 manifest half-cell specific capacitances of 193.8 F g −1 and 123.6 F g −1 @ current density of 4 A g −1, respectively. The introduction of CQDs into the Co3O4 matrix increased the charge-transfer process, which is primarily responsible for the improved electrochemical capabilities of the nanocomposite. The hybrid asymmetric supercapacitor delivers an energy density of 2.49 W h kg−1 @ a power density of 426 W kg−2 with a capacitance retention of 81.8%.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The shortage of conventional energy sources, and their health, and environmental issues have made the research on innovative sustainable, environmentally friendly materials for energy production and storage, inevitable to achieve the growing energy demand [1]. In recent years, fuel cells and batteries have addressed our planet's essential energy requirements [2]; whereas, conventional capacitors and batteries are the main technologies that consistently store and deliver energy. However, conventional capacitors lack in energy density; but deliver high power density whereas, batteries, offer high energy density with low power density [1,3]. Supercapacitors (SCs), are a new development in electrochemical energy storage systems which are considered to be intermittent of conventional capacitors and batteriesand can provide a considerable energy and power density [4]. Based on their energy-storing processes, SCs can be categorized into two types: electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), which store energy by the formation of charge layers near the electrolyte-electrode interface, and pseudocapacitors (PSCs) that store energy by redox reactions [5,6]. A hybrid-type supercapacitor uses both EDLC and PSC so that,the synergistic effect of high power and energy density results in enhanced cell voltage [7,8]. The SCs comprising non-Faradaic components (negative electrode materials) such as carbonaceous substances and Faradaic components (positive electrode materials) such as metal oxides, metal nitrates, metal sulfides etc. are known as asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs). Hence, the combination of non-Faradaic and Faradaic elements confers the ASCs an improved energy density with high power.

Transition metal oxides (TMOs) and transition metal sulfides are widely employed as potential redox active battery-type substances because of their disparate valence states and fast-reversible redox reactions [9]. Metal oxides have occupied a very important position in electroactive materials. Transition metal oxides such as NiO, Co3O4, and MnO2 have a high specific capacitance due to their wide working voltage range and diverse oxidation levels, but their efficiency is limited by poor stability and low electron mobility [10,11]. Co-based materials have introduced themselves as propitious active electrode materials for supercapacitors, owing to their high abundance, inexpensive, environmental compatibility, and great electronic conductivity [12]. Among Co-based materials, cobalt oxide (Co3O4), as an important transition metal oxide, has captivated peculiar recognition and been extensively analyzed as a battery-type material for supercapacitor applications, owing to its prospects as a propitious substitution for RuO2, its high theoretical capacitance (3560 F g −1), cheap price, practical availability, and eco-friendly nature [13,14]. Co3O4 is a pleasant active material because it can show high specific capacitance and excellent electrochemical performance. However, most reports show that the practical capacitances of TMOs are less than theoretical values, because of their relatively large particle size [15]. For example, Li et al., using the low-temperature solution process at 70 °C prepared Co3O4 nanoparticles and used them as a supercapacitor electrode which displayed a specific capacitance of 304 F g −1 at 5 mV s −1 [16]. Likewise, Co3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized from cobalt nitrate by Jianget al., in which the prepared Co3O4 exhibited a specific capacitance of 189.1 F g −1 at 0.2 A g −1 [17]. Manteghiet al. prepared Co3O4 electrodes from cobalt chloride with F-127 as a surfactant to control the particle size. The prepared sample demonstrated a specific capacitance of 351 F g −1 at 0.85 A g −1 [18].

The addition of conducting carbon-based substances is one of the favorable options to enhance the transmittance of the metal oxide and sulfide electrode materials. Carbon materials such as activated carbon, graphene [19], carbon nanotube [20], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [21] and graphene quantum dots [22] have demonstrated encouraging results in the refinement of electrochemical properties of the electrode substances. CQDs are a quasi-zero-dimensional new group of carbon nanosubstance with sizes below 10 nm [23]. They are readily soluble in water and have higher chemical stability and a high functionalization potential. CQDs are a viable preference for catalysis, sensing [24] and supercapacitor applications [25] due to their exceptional photochemical and electrochemical capabilities. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) not only possess plentiful functional groups on the surface withunique chemical and physical properties but, also manifest good conductivity. The rapid electron transfer property of CQDs along with its ability to form composites makes it easier to refine the electrochemical properties of the other substances [26,27]. For instance, Jian et al. reported the construction of Carbon quantum reinforced polypyrrole nanowire (PPy-NW) was constructed via electrostatic self-assembly strategy. As prepared composite exhibited a superior electrochemical properties with specific capacitance of 306 F g − 1 @ current density of 0.5 A g −1 [28]. Luo et al. demonstrated the improvement in the conductivity of NiCo-LDHs by anchoring with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on carbon cloth (CC) for supercapacitor application. The CQDs/NiCo-LDHs@CCelectrode delivered large reversible specific capacitance of 2023 F g −1 (5.22 F cm−2) at 5 mA cm−2 in 6 M KOH and exhibited superior rate capability with 84.3% capacitance retention at 30 mA cm−2 with excellent cycle life [29]. Pallavolu et al. derived a N and S doped CQDs from lemon juice and encapsulated with graphene sheet and studied its electrochemical properties for supercapacitor application. Prepared electrode materials displayed a battery like behavior with specific capacity 284 mAh/g at current densities of 2 mA. The enhancement in specific capacity is due to the existence of large number of redox active O-/N-/S-containing functional groups within the graphitic network of the CQDs-graphene composite [30].

In this work, we prepared pure Co3O4 (PCO) and fabricated Co3O4/CQDs (COC) nanocomposite using a simple hydrothermal technique. The electrochemical performance in the three-electrode system is appreciable in the case of composite materials compared to pure Co3O4, and the impact of the incorporation of CQDs inside the Co3O4 matrix on the enhancement of electrochemical accomplishments is discussed in this manuscript. Besides, increasing the ionic mobility of the composite substance, the CQDs potentially improve the specific capacitance of the composite by providing additional positive charges into the matrix [31]. Further, the existence of CQDs may lower the surface area of the porous substances by permeating into the pores [32]. Further, a hybrid ASC was constructed by incorporating the fabricated COC composite as the positive electrode and reduced graphene oxide (r-GO) deposited on the NF as the negative electrode.
Analytical grade urea (CO(NH2)2), cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O), and ethanol (C2H5OH). The chemicals were bought from Merck (India) Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Co3O4nanoparticles and Co3O4/CQD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the Co3O4 nanoparticles and the Co3O4/CQDsnanocomposite are schematically demonstrated in Fig. 1 . In this synthesis process of Co3O4 nanoparticles, 1.125 mole CO(NH2)2 and 0.5 mole Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O were dissolved in 70 ml of distilled water under magnetic stirring [33]. The acquired solution was then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and managed hydrothermally at 180 °C for 12 h before being reduced to room temperature automatically.

The final products, pure Co3O4 (PCO) nanoparticles, were collected, rinsed numerous times with deionized water and ethanol, and then annealed in flowing argon at 300 °C for 1 hour.The loaded weight of the active material was 1 mg cm−2. The primary chemical reactions involved in the formation mechanism of the PCO nanoparticles through hydrothermal synthesis can be represented as follows: [33,34]
(1)
C O
(
N
H 2
)
2
→ N
H 3
+ H N C O
(2)
C
o
2 +
+ 6 N
H 3
↼
⇁
[
C o
(
N
H 3
)
6
]
2 +
(3)
[
C o
(
N
H 3
)
6
]
2 +
+ 2 O
H
−
→ C o
(
O H
)
2
+ 6 N
H 3
(4)
6 C o
(
O H
)
2
+
O 2
→ 2 C
o 3
O 4
+ 6
H 2
O

In the synthesis of Co3O4/CQD nanocomposite, the same procedure for synthesis of Co3O4 was adopted with addition of previously synthesis 2 ml of colloidal CQDs [35] with the Co-precursors. Finally, the sample was named as COC.

The crystal parameters and structure of hydrothermally prepared samples were investigated by powder X-ray diffractometer Rigaku D/Max-2500 and used in the X-ray source of Cu Kα radiation (wavelength λ =0.1542 nm). The surface morphology of the electrodes was visualized by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM Model is ZEISS-EVO 18 Research) having the provision of Energy dispersive X-Ray spectrometer (EDX) (BRUKER-X Flash 6130). TEM measurements were performed with a JEOL JEM-2010 at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The oxidation states and binding energy of NiCoS/NFs were scrutinized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (XPS Modal is PHI 5000 Versa probe).

The Ni foam (NF) substrate with a dimension of 3.0 × 1.0 cm was ultrasonicated with 3 mol of diluted hydrochloric acid, acetone and ethanol for 20 min each. It was then washed with deionized water to eliminate oxide films and completely dried overnight at60 °C. The working electrode was assembled by the overlaying of the semi-liquid mixture of PCO or COC, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), acetylene carbon black with a weight ratio of 8: 1: 1 and 0.5 ml of N-methylproline (NMP) solution over an area of 1 cm2 on the substrate (pre-cleaned Ni foam) and then made to dry at 100 °C in vacuum for 12 h. Electrochemical studies were performed in a three-electrode system with 1 M KOH aqueous solution as the electrolyte. The Ag/AgCl and platinum wire were employed as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. All electrochemical tests were performed in an electrochemical apparatus (SP-150, Bio-Logic Science Instruments-France). Cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GC/GD) tests were carried out in the applied potential span of 0 to 0.4 at various applied voltages and current densities. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study is carried out in an open circuit with an ac potential of 5 mV amplitude and an applied frequency stretch of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz.

A solid-state asymmetric supercapacitor was constructed using r-GO/NF (negative) and the preparedCOC coated on NF (positive) as electrodes, respectively; while the pre-prepared PVA-KOH hydrogel acted as a solid-state electrolyte. To prepare the negative electrode, 30 mg of graphene oxide was mixed with 70 ml of distilled water and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 90 min [11,12].The Ni foam (prepared in Section 2.4) and the graphene oxide mixture were taken in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a 100 ml capacity. The autoclave was tightly closed and heated at 180 °C for 2 h in a closed oven, after which it was made to cool to the surrounding temperature spontaneously. Graphene oxide was reduced on Ni foam. The Ni foams coated with r-GO nanosheets were collected from the autoclave and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol before being fully dried overnight in a vacuum at a temperature of 60 °C and then designated as NF@r-GO (0.15 mg cm−2) and used as a negative electrode for ASC. To optimize the charge between the two electrode, mass of the electrodes was balanced by C − × m − × V − = C + × m + × V +, where C − and C + are the specific capacitances of negative and positive electrodes, V − and V + are the potential window of the negative and positive electrodes, m − and m + are the loading mass of the electrodes. The optimized mass loading of the two electrode was 1.15 mg. The PVA-KOH solid-state electrolyte was evenly applied to the anode and cathode surfaces before being fitted into the two thin Pt disks (the current collector mode is a Swagelok-type cell) to build an ASC. Finally, the electrochemical performance of the assembled ASC was studied on the BioLogic SP-150 workstation.

The crystalline nature and phase purity of synthesized PCO and COC were determined by XRD analysis (Fig. 2 ). The intense spectral lines situated at 33.23°, 37.13°, 45.17°, 59.78°, and 66.36° are diffracted from the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) planes of the cubic Co3O4 structure, which is compatible with the typical card JCPDS card no: 073–1701 [36].

No other diffraction peaks from impurities are detected, suggesting the formation of pure cobalt oxide. The narrow diffraction peaks imply the effective crystallization of the material. However, due to the small concentration of CQDs in the COC nanocomposite, the characteristic spectral lines of CQDs are not clearly shown in the XRD spectra of the COC composite. Hence, the diffraction pattern of the COC nanocomposite material matches that of the PCO.

XPS investigation is employed to study the elemental composition of the COC. The survey spectra reveal the presence of spectral lines about the components Co, O and C (Fig. 3 a). The fitted peaks at 780.83 and 796.65 eV shown in Fig. 3b could be designated to be 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 respectively of cobalt 2p region [37]. The other two peaks represent the shake-up satellites. Likewise, in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), the peak at 531.2 eV was accredited to metal-oxygen bonding coming from Co3O4 [38]. The deconvolution of the C1s signal discloses three peaks at 283.97, 285.42 and 287.12 eV (Fig. 3d), representing the C = C/ CC (sp2 graphitic structure), CO, and C = O groups, respectively [20]. FESEM images of PCO [Fig. 4 (a)&(b)] and COC [Fig. 4(c) & (d)] at two different resolutions are depicted in Fig. 4. From Fig. 4(a) and (b), it is obvious that the structure of PCO comprises countless irregular spherical morphology with a rough surface. Further, the irregularly shaped spherical possess clear boundaries between each other and the average size of the Co3O4 nanoparticlesis found to be 47 nm. Fig. 4(c) and (d) show images of the COC possessing a rough surface with numerous smaller-sized nanodots. Unlike POC, the nanoparticles are found to be agglomerated without clear boundaries which might be due to the presence of CQDs along with Co3O4 in COC. The CQDs are known to possess numerous functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxylic acid, amine etc. at their surface. The presence of these organic functional groups over its surface makes the CQDs negatively charged, which in turn combines with cobalt ions electrostatically, thus promoting heterogeneous nucleation of Co3O4. The electrostatic interaction between the multiple functional groups of CQDs and cobalt ions of Co3O4 results in the aggregation of nanoparticles. A similar aggregation has been reported with CQDs@CaCO3 nanocomposites by Qiu et al. [39]. The presence of CQDs is capable of modifying the morphology and crystal structure of metallic compounds.

The mapping images and EDX analysis of COC represented in Fig. 5 (a)-(e) substantiate the formation of Co, O and C elements. From Fig. 5 (a-d), it is obvious that the elements in nanocomposites are uniformly distributed throughout the nanocomposites.

The HRTEM analysis was employed to further investigate the morphological features of the COC. Fig. 6 (a)-(c) represents the HRTEM images of COC nanocompostes at various magnifications. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic investigation substantiates the Co3O4 nanoparticles and the presence of carbon quantum dots. The CQDs were differentiated from Co3O4 by round marking as shown in Fig. 6b. The average particle size of Co3O4 is 13 nm and the average size of CQDs is 5 nm. The clear lattice fringes in Fig. 6c further substantiate its crystalline quality, as evinced by XRD investigation.

Furthermore, the interplanar distances (311), (220), and (111) with observed lattice spacing of 0.245, 0.288, and 0.462 nm are in good agreement with the cubic phase of Co3O4. The well-defined concentric diffraction rings displayed in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Fig. 6d suggest the polycrystalline nature of Co3O4. These ring patterns were assigned to (111), (220), (311), (422) and (440) reflections of the face-centered cubic Co3O4 structure (JCPDS no.73–1701) [40]. The d-spacing of 0.21 nm represents the (100) lattice planes of graphitic carbon. The coexistence of the planes of Co3O4 and the (100) plane of CQDs indicates the formation of the COC composite [41]. EDX analysis (Fig. 6e) exhibits the existence of Co, O and C elements; the Cu and C (partly from CQDs) arise from the carbon–copper grid.

Electrochemical studies in three-electrode configurations were performed to evaluate the viability of PCO and COC as SC electrodes. Fig. 7 a and b represents the CVs of PCO and COC respectively. The CVs manifest two oxidation and two reduction peaks which are from the redox processes of Co3O4/CoOOH/CoO2. The Faradaic capacitive behavior emerges from the preceding redox reactions of Co3O4 [33,42]: The first redox pair corresponds to the transformation between Co3O4 and CoOOH:
(5)
C
o 3
O 4
+ O
H
−
+
H 2
O ⇌ 3 C o O O H +
e
−

Also, the second redox couple is of the reversible reaction between CoOOH and CoO2, denoted by the following expression:
(6)
C o O O H + O
H
−
⇌ C o
O 2
+
H 2
O +
e
−

The redox peaks can be attributed to charge diffusion in the electrolyte, implying that the produced electrodes manifest a battery-type electrode [43]. The apparent redox peaks at high scan rates suggest a high rate of competency and excellent electrode reversibility.

Fig. 7e displays the comparative CV of synthesized nanomaterials at a scan rate of 60 mV s −1. The CVs of COC display a greater electrode peak current density with a measured integrated area of 3.29 which is larger compared to PCO with an integrated area of 1.75. It manifests the incorporation of CQDs enables a better charge transfer in the case of COC.

The GC/GD curves of PCO and COC are displayed in Fig. 7c and d, respectively. The symmetrical GC/GD curves show that the electrodes exhibit excellent electrochemical reproducibility and charge-discharge capabilities. The nonlinear [44], plateau-shaped GC/GD plots indicate their PSC or battery-like behavior that matches with the CVs. Fig. 7f illustrates the comparative GC/GD curves of PCO and COC. Evidently, the COC has a larger area, implying enhanced efficiency, which matches with the CVs [45,46] and the specific capacitance was calculated by following the equation:
(7)
C s
=
I ×
Δ
t
Δ
m × V
Where I is applied current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), Δm is the active mass of the electrode (g), V is the potential window (V). The specific capacitance calculated for COC is greater than the specific capacitance for PCO.

The specific capacitance values of the two electrodes are compared in Fig. 8 a. The specific capacitances of COC and PCO at a current density of 4 A g −1 are 193.8 F g −1 and 123.6 F g −1, respectively. The capacitances of PCO and COC are larger than that of earlier assessed works such as MnO2, Fe2O3 electrodes [47], the MnO2/ZnOcore–shell nanorod array [48]. Notably, the specific capacitance of the COC is greater than that of the PCO at a similar scan rate. The incorporation of CQDs into the matrix of PCO improves the electrochemical capabilities of PCO for the following reasons: (1) the abundance of functional groups on the surface of CQDs makes it much easier to form composites with other materials; (2) the small size of CQDs confers a large surface area for more convenient electrochemical reaction; and (3) the rapid electron transfer property of CQDs, which improves the composite's charge transfer rate [49]. The consistent decrease of capacitance with the increase in the current density is attributed to the increment in IR drop due to the intrinsic resistance of active substances and insufficient Faraday reaction at high current densities.
The COC was investigated for 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 10 A g −1. The COC manifest a Coulombic efficiency of 97.6% with capacitance retention of 83.6% after 5000 cycles (Fig. 8d)
An ASC has been constructed using NF@r-GO as a cathode, COC as an anode and KOH/PVA as a solid-state electrolyte. Fig. 9 a depicts the energy-storing process of the constructed COC// NF@r-GO.

When charging or discharging occur, the cations and anions in the electrolyte proceed towards the cathode and anode, respectively. The establishment of layers of charges near the cathode leads to EDLC characteristics and interchange of electrons between anions and anode initiates the battery type characteristics. It is found that the asymmetric device with a combination of the potential windows of NF@r-GO (−1.0 to 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl) and COC (0 to 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl) can hold a high operating voltage of 1.4 V (Fig. 9b). The cyclic voltammetry of the constructed ASC was evaluated at various operating potentials at constant current density (Fig. 9c) and at various scan rates (Fig. 9d). The quasi rectangle shaped CVs implies the combined effect of PSC based electrode of the battery type and the EDLC electrode. The consistency of design of CV curves at different scan rates and cell voltages implies the good capacitive performance of the ASC with no hydrogen or oxygen evolution. The GC/GD characteristics of ASC displayed in Fig. 9e & f at different device potentials and current densities manifest nearly linear profiles at all current densities. When increasing the scan rates, the shape of the CV curves can still be reproduced, evidencing the excellent reversible capability of the device. The specific capacitance of the ASC was calculated from Eq. (7). The specific capacitance of COC// NF@r-GO is 9.15 F g−1 at a current density of 0.6 A g − 1. The change in specific capacitance with the change in current density is displayed in Fig. 10 a, which shows the consistent decrease of capacitance with the increase in the current density. This tendency is attributed to the increment in IR drop due to the intrinsic resistance of active substances and insufficient Faraday reaction at high current densities. Moreover, the capacitance is derived from both storage methods, a combination of a battery-type or PSC-based electrode and an EDLC-based electrode. In the literature [50,51], this category of ASC device is referred to as hybrid supercapacitors.

The volumetric energy and powder densities of ASC were calculated using Eqs. (8) and (9).
(8)
E
=
1
2
×
3.6
C
V
2
(9)
P =
E
Δ
t
×
3600
Where E is the energy density (W h kg−1), C is the capacitance (F g −1) of the ASC, V is the cell voltage (V) of the ASC, P is the power density (W kg−1), Δt is discharge time (s).

The Ragone plot comparing the power and energy densities of the device appears in Fig. 10b. The energy and power densities of the ASC at a current density of 0.6 A g −1are 2.49 W h kg−1 and 426 W kg−1 respectively.

The electrochemical conductive properties of COC//NF@r-GO were assessed by the EIS study and Fig. 10c displays the Nyquist curve. The circuit parameters Rs and Rct calculated using an equivalent circuit (Fig. 10d) are 0.51 Ω and 0.23 Ω respectively. Further, the cyclic durability of the device was investigated at a high current density of 10 A g −1 for repeated 5000 charge-discharge cycles. The COC retains 81.8% of its initial capacitance after persistent 5000 charge-discharge cycles with a Coulombic efficiency of 97.88% (Fig. 10e). An LED lit up by COC//NF@r-GO is displayed in Fig. 10f.

In summary, a pure Co3O4 (PCO) was synthesized using a simple hydrothermal technique and CQDs were successfully anchored on Co3O4. The quasi-zero dimension of CQDs confers a large surface area for a more convenient electrochemical reaction. Furthermore, CQDs increase the conductance of the COC by enabling faster charge transfer. The electrochemical performance of PCO and COC exhibited excellent battery-like traits. The COC showed the highest specific capacitance of 193.8 F cm−2 at 4 A g −1with capacitance retention of 83.6% after 5000 cycles. The COC showed better electrochemical accomplishments, such as a lower charge transfer resistance, a larger integrated area of CVs, and enhanced specific capacitance compared to PCO. The assembled ASC manifested a maximum cell voltage (1.4 V) with high energy and power densities of 2.49 W h kg−1 and 426 W kg−1, respectively. These encouraging characteristics, when combined, give our COC//NF@r-GO a great prospect for practical applications in future energy storage systems.
ASC Device and Characteristics

Periyasami Prabakaran: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Gowdhaman Arumugam: Writing – review & editing. Perumal Ramu: Visualization. Manickam Selvaraj: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Mohammed A. Assiri: Funding acquisition. Samuel Lalthazuala Rokhum: Funding acquisition. Sivakumar Periyasamy: Visualization. Silambarasan Arjunan: Project administration. Ramesh Rajendran: Supervision.
